name: Probability — Sample Space, Events and the Addition Rule
whatIsIt: The SAMPLE SPACE is the set of ALL possible outcomes of an experiment (rolling a die = {1,2,3,4,5,6}). An EVENT is a subset of those outcomes. For equally likely outcomes, P(event) = favourable ÷ total, always between 0 and 1. Rules: a sure event has P = 1, an impossible one P = 0, the complement P(A′) = 1 − P(A), and the addition rule P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B).
whyNeeded: Probability measures uncertainty precisely — games, weather forecasts, insurance, medical tests, and quality checks all rest on it. The sample-space view and the rules let you handle 'A or B' and 'not A' correctly.
realLifeUses: Games of chance — dice, cards, spinners
Judging the reliability of a medical test

vocabulary.meaning: The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment.
A chosen subset of the sample space (like 'an even number').
The probability that A does NOT happen: P(A′) = 1 − P(A).
P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B) (subtract the overlap once).

story.title: The Fair Dice Game
story.text: Zoya rolls a die. 'What's the chance of an even number?' asks Nana. She lists the sample space {1,2,3,4,5,6} — six outcomes. The evens are {2,4,6} — three favourable. So P(even) = 3 ÷ 6 = 1/2. 'And a number from 1 to 6 is SURE, so P = 1, while a 7 is impossible, P = 0,' Nana adds. 'Probability always sits between 0 and 1.'
story.extractedProblem: Rolling a die, what is the probability of an even number?
story.answerInStory: 1/2 — three evens out of six outcomes: 3/6 = 1/2.

visual.caption: Six equally likely outcomes; three are even. The shaded fraction 3/6 = 1/2 is the probability of an even number.

teachingGallery.title: Favourable over total
teachingGallery.note: Count the sample space, count the favourable outcomes, divide.

examples.caption: An event and its complement fill the whole space: P(A) + P(A′) = 1.

standardMethod.summary: List the sample space. P(event) = favourable ÷ total (between 0 and 1). Use P(A′) = 1 − P(A) for 'not', and P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B) for 'or'.
steps: Write the sample space — every equally likely outcome.
Count the favourable outcomes for your event.
P(event) = favourable ÷ total (it must be between 0 and 1).
For 'A or B', use P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B) (subtract the overlap once).

workedExamples.problem: How many outcomes are in the sample space of rolling one die?
If P(A) = 1/4, find P(A′) (A does not happen).

commonMistakes.mistake: Getting a probability bigger than 1 (or negative).
commonMistakes.fix: Probability is always between 0 and 1. If you get more than 1, you counted wrong — favourable can't exceed total.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) and forgetting to subtract the overlap.
commonMistakes.fix: Subtract the shared part once: P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B). Only if A and B can't both happen is the overlap 0.

easy.q: How many outcomes are in the sample space of one die?
The probability of a SURE event is?

medium.q: Probability of an even number on a die = 3/6 = ? (write like 1/2)

challenge.q: The probability of an IMPOSSIBLE event is?
For 'A or B', Ravi writes P(A) + P(B) and gets more than 1. What's the fix?

options.label: Subtract the overlap: P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B)

challenge.answer: Subtract the overlap: P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B)

questions.q: Outcomes in the sample space of one coin?
P(head) on a fair coin? (write like 1/2)

teachBackPrompt: Roll a die for a grown-up and list the sample space {1..6}. Show P(even) = 3/6 = 1/2, that a sure event is 1 and an impossible one is 0, and explain P(A′) = 1 − P(A).

revisionCard.summary: Sample space = all outcomes; event = a subset. P(event) = favourable ÷ total, always between 0 and 1. Sure = 1, impossible = 0. Complement: P(A′) = 1 − P(A). Addition rule: P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B) (subtract the overlap once).

rememberIt.hook: Three rules: P >= 0, P(sure) = 1, add for mutually exclusive
rememberIt.unpack: Every probability is between 0 and 1; the whole sample space totals 1; for events that cannot both happen, P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B).

studentQuestions.a: Probability built from a few basic rules rather than just counting, so everything stays consistent.
The set of ALL possible outcomes. Its total probability is always 1.
